free_responses.M1:
# Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and Ecosystem Science

The agency should make microbial and ecosystem science its primary decadal priority. This is the area where a national funder's dollar has the highest marginal return, because the field is scientifically ripe, structurally under-funded, and sits upstream of nearly every other priority on the list — climate (soil and ocean carbon cycling), health (human microbiome, antimicrobial resistance, zoonotic spillover), and food security (nitrogen-fixing microbes, crop resilience). Sequencing, single-cell methods, and ML-driven ecology have matured to the point that systematic discovery is now possible; what's missing is sustained, coordinated funding at national scale. That is precisely what a $2.5–3B/year commitment can change, and what private capital will not do, because returns are diffuse public goods.

The most compelling alternative is Option A, climate-and-energy. I reject it not because it is unimportant but because it is the area *least* in need of this agency's marginal dollar. Climate science is already the beneficiary of massive global investment — IRA-scale industrial policy, EU Green Deal, mission-driven energy agencies, philanthropic billions. A national basic-science funder adding another 35% weight here buys incrementalism. By contrast, microbial ecology is where climate mitigation will actually be *operationalized* over the next decade (soil carbon, ocean biological pump, methane-cycling microbes), and almost no one is funding the foundational work at the scale required. Choosing E is in fact the highest-leverage climate bet available, plus much more.

I also set aside Option D: AI safety is consequential, but frontier capability and alignment work is concentrating in well-capitalized private labs whose access to models and compute the agency cannot match. A public funder is better positioned to support the *external* social-science evaluation of AI, which can be sustained at maintenance levels rather than as the flagship.

**2036 success criterion:** a national soil and marine microbiome reference atlas comparable in stature to the human genome project; at least two scaled agricultural interventions (microbial inoculants or breeding-for-microbiome traits) demonstrating ≥10% yield or fertilizer-reduction gains across major crops; and quantified, monitored microbial contributions to national carbon accounting. If those exist, the bet paid off — and every other priority on this list benefits from the foundation laid.
free_responses.M2:
I recommend making **AI safety and computational social science** the primary national priority for the decade.

The decisive reason is leverage. Advanced AI is likely to become a general-purpose infrastructure for science, industry, education, defense, media, and government within the review period—not in some distant future. If these systems remain poorly understood, unevaluated, and governed mainly by private incentives, the country risks large-scale failures: labor-market disruption without adaptation, democratic manipulation, brittle deployment in critical systems, concentration of economic power, and potentially unsafe autonomous capabilities. A public research agency has a distinctive role here: to build the independent scientific base that private labs are not structurally incentivized to provide—interpretability, alignment methods, robust evaluations, audit infrastructure, secure model testing environments, and empirical social science on real-world impacts.

The strongest alternative is climate-and-energy science. Its urgency is undeniable, and it should remain strongly supported. But climate is already a mature global funding priority with large public, philanthropic, and industrial investment streams; many of the binding constraints are now deployment, regulation, permitting, finance, and political coordination. AI, by contrast, is moving faster than public understanding, and the institutional capacity to evaluate and govern it is dangerously underbuilt. A decade of elevated funding could turn a reactive policy environment into a scientifically grounded one.

This priority should not mean funding speculative commentary about AI. It should mean hard technical and empirical work: scalable interpretability, adversarial evaluation, model monitoring, privacy-preserving auditing, human-AI interaction, labor-market measurement, misinformation resilience, and governance mechanisms tested against real systems.

By 2036, this bet would be a clear success if the country has a trusted public AI evaluation infrastructure used by regulators, courts, firms, and international partners; if major AI systems can be independently tested for dangerous capabilities, bias, deception, cyber misuse, and reliability before deployment; and if evidence-based adaptation policies have measurably reduced harms to workers, students, public institutions, and vulnerable groups. The goal is not to slow science, but to make the most powerful technology of the era scientifically legible and socially governable.
free_responses.M3:
I strongly recommend **Option E: Microbial and ecosystem science** as the agency’s primary funding priority for the next decade. This domain offers the highest marginal return on public investment because it sits at the intersecting root of our most acute crises—climate change, global health, and food security—while remaining chronically under-capitalized relative to its potential.

Public research funding is most transformative when it builds foundational knowledge that private markets cannot easily capture and monetize. While Option A (Climate-and-energy science) is the most compelling alternative given our planetary urgency, macro-climate technology and energy transitions are already experiencing a massive influx of private venture capital and targeted industrial policy. Dedicating up to 40% of the agency’s basic science budget to an already crowded climate space risks yielding diminishing, incremental returns. Similarly, AI and biomedicine are already flooded with private billions. 

Conversely, the vast economic and survival benefits of ecosystem services and microbiology are profound but diffuse, making them the perfect candidate for a massive public science push. By decoding human, soil, and ocean microbiomes, we unlock non-incremental breakthroughs: drought-resistant crops, natural carbon sequestration multipliers, and novel antibiotics. We are currently scratching the surface of a biological frontier as vast as foundational physics was a century ago, but with much faster translational impacts. Because microbial systems have immense cross-cutting influence, breakthroughs here will indirectly accelerate both climate adaptation and human longevity. 

By 2036, this decade-long bet will be a definitive success if the agency’s investments yield a comprehensive, predictive model of foundational microbiomes that directly enables a breakthrough in planetary adaptation. Specifically, a clear triumph would be the deployment of engineered soil microbial therapies that replace 30% of global synthetic fertilizer use while measurably increasing agricultural carbon drawdown. Delivering this would be a generational environmental and geopolitical victory, proving the agency boldly recognized the microscopic foundation of our macroeconomic future.
free_responses.M4:
The agency should select **Biomedical Engineering and Aging Biology** as its primary funding priority. The defining challenge of the 21st century is not a single crisis, but a slow-motion demographic revolution: societies worldwide are aging, and healthspan still trails far behind lifespan. Even modest progress—delaying the onset of multimorbidity by one to two years—would generate trillions in economic value and immeasurable human benefit. This is the highest-certainty, highest-return investment available, and its moment has arrived. Advances in single-cell biology, gene editing, organ-on-chip platforms, and geroscience have matured to the point where targeted, elevated funding can translate decades of basic insight into therapies that alter how we age. By 2036, success will be visible in the form of at least two approved interventions explicitly targeting hallmarks of aging—such as senolytic drugs or epigenetic reprogramming—with population-level evidence that healthy lifespan in high-risk groups has measurably expanded. That outcome would validate the bet and permanently reorient clinical medicine toward prevention at the deepest level.

The most compelling alternative, AI safety and computational social science, addresses a transformative technology but suffers from an immature research community and massive private-sector duplication. We cannot yet reliably evaluate grant quality in a field where the core methods are contested, and the risk of redundant or prematurely deployed funds is high. Aging biology, by contrast, has a deep bench of experienced investigators, clear translational milestones, and a funding gap that private capital alone cannot fill because the path to market often requires long, expensive trials for preventive indications. Ethical concerns about longevity disparity are real, but they are best addressed through active public investment that shapes equitable access, rather than ceding the field entirely to private enclaves. This priority delivers a concrete, timeline-defined return that taxpayers can rally behind: a generation of therapies that keep people vigorous into their 80s and beyond. That is not incremental—it is civilizationally decisive.
free_responses.M5:
I recommend making **AI safety and computational social science** the agency’s primary priority for the decade. Artificial intelligence is not merely another research domain—it is the metascientific substrate rapidly reshaping how climate models are run, diseases are treated, fundamental physics is conducted, and ecosystems are monitored. Betting the decade on AI understanding and governance is therefore the highest-leverage move available, because progress here amplifies or constrains every other portfolio. The private sector is pouring billions into capabilities; what it systematically underfunds is measurement, interpretability, and the social science of how these systems reconfigure labor markets, democratic discourse, and scientific authority itself. A major public commitment can build the intellectual infrastructure that market incentives will not.

The most compelling alternative is climate-and-energy science, given its planetary urgency. But that field already commands substantial global investment and enjoys mature institutional pathways; maintained at current levels, it will continue to advance. By contrast, AI safety and computational social science lack established senior faculty pipelines, validated evaluation regimes, and shared research infrastructure. A decade of elevated funding can bootstrap this discipline from a fledgling, fragmented community into a rigorous, evidence-based field before algorithmic systems become further locked into critical societal infrastructure.

Success by 2036 should be measured by a concrete, society-facing outcome: the establishment of a federally validated science of AI evaluation that produces peer-reviewed interpretability benchmarks and real-time societal-impact audits for high-stakes AI deployments. If, by 2036, major AI systems in healthcare, criminal justice, and public discourse can be subjected to transparent, third-party stress-testing grounded in publicly funded research—and if associated governance frameworks demonstrably prevent measurable algorithmic harm—then this bet will have returned its investment many times over, not in a single sector, but across the entirety of civic and scientific life.

free_responses.M1:
# Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and Ecosystem Science

The strongest case for a national funding agency's elevated priority is not the field with the largest constituency or the loudest urgency, but the field where a marginal public dollar buys the most under-captured scientific return. Microbial and ecosystem science meets that test better than any alternative on the table.

Microbiomes — human, soil, marine — sit at the causal root of problems the other priorities only address downstream. Soil microbial communities determine agricultural productivity and a substantial fraction of terrestrial carbon flux; ocean microbes drive roughly half the planet's primary production; the human microbiome is increasingly implicated in metabolic, immune, and neurological disease. Yet relative to its leverage, the field is dramatically underfunded, methodologically fragmented, and largely uncaptured by private R&D — exactly the profile where public agencies have comparative advantage. Sequencing, single-cell, and ML-driven functional annotation have matured to the point that a coordinated decade of investment could move the field from descriptive cataloguing to predictive, engineerable ecology.

The most compelling alternative is Option A, climate-and-energy. I reject it as the *primary* priority precisely because it is already the best-funded mission science globally; an additional national surge produces less marginal insight, and much of the remaining bottleneck is deployment and policy, not science. Critically, microbial science *is* climate science in its highest-leverage form — soil carbon, methane cycling, ocean biogeochemistry, and resilient agriculture all run through microbial mechanisms. Option B (aging) is already drawing massive private capital; Option C (foundational physics) has a return horizon incompatible with a 10-year strategic bet; Option D (AI safety) is moving faster inside frontier labs than any grant cycle can track.

**Success metric by 2036:** a validated, mechanistic, predictive framework linking soil microbial composition to carbon sequestration and crop yield at field scale, deployed across at least three major agricultural regions, plus two clinically approved microbiome-based therapeutics and a national biodiversity-genomics baseline enabling early detection of ecosystem collapse. That combination would deliver measurable climate, health, and food-security returns simultaneously — the kind of cross-cutting payoff no other priority can credibly promise from a single decade of concentrated investment.
free_responses.M2:
I recommend **Option D: AI safety and computational social science** as the primary priority for the decade.

AI is the one candidate technology likely to reshape every other scientific, economic, and political domain within the review period itself. Unlike foundational physics, its consequences are not mainly distant; unlike biomedicine, they are not confined to one sector; unlike climate, they are moving faster than existing public institutions can understand or govern. A national funding agency has a distinctive role here: to build the independent scientific base that private AI labs have neither the incentives nor the legitimacy to provide. Interpretability, robustness, model evaluation, human-AI interaction, labor-market effects, democratic resilience, and governance design are public goods. If they are left primarily to firms racing for capability, society will be regulating opaque systems with inadequate evidence.

The strongest alternative is climate-and-energy science, because the stakes are planetary and urgent. But climate already has a mature research ecosystem, major international funding streams, and a clearer pathway through energy deployment, industrial policy, and regulation. It should remain strongly supported, but the marginal public-research dollar is likely more transformative in AI safety and societal impact, where standards, methods, institutions, and talent pipelines are still being formed. A decade of elevated funding could define the field before commercial incentives harden around less accountable models.

By 2036, this choice would be a clear success if the country has established a trusted public infrastructure for AI assurance: reproducible evaluation suites for frontier models, interpretability tools that can identify dangerous capabilities and failure modes before deployment, empirically grounded governance standards adopted by regulators and international partners, and longitudinal evidence on AI’s effects on labor, education, health, inequality, and democracy. The goal is not to slow useful AI, but to make powerful AI systems scientifically legible, governable, and socially beneficial. If successful, this priority would not merely fund a discipline; it would help society retain agency over the most general-purpose technology of the century.
free_responses.M3:
I strongly recommend designating **Option E (Microbial and ecosystem science)** as the primary funding priority for the next decade. 

Microbial and ecosystem science represents the ultimate "missing middle" of modern inquiry. It operates at the critical nexus of human health, agricultural productivity, and climate stability, yet remains chronically underfunded relative to its potential. Because ecosystem services and public-health microbiomes are diffuse public goods, their economic returns are systemic rather than easily captured by a single corporate patent. Consequently, private capital will not adequately fund this space. Public funding is uniquely positioned to step in, requiring bold agency leadership to orchestrate the foundational, cross-disciplinary infrastructure needed to turn a historically fragmented community into a high-leverage scientific powerhouse. 

The most compelling alternative is Option A (Climate-and-energy science). While the urgency of the climate crisis is undeniable, climate science is already a heavily capitalized, crowded space. Channeling 30-40% of the agency’s budget into standard climate modeling or energy materials risks yielding only incremental gains while heavily exposing the agency to political polarization. In contrast, Option E provides a highly effective, politically insulated "backdoor" to climate adaptation. Soil health and ocean microbiology hold the largely untapped keys to organic carbon sequestration and agricultural resilience, accelerating climate goals without duplicating existing public-private climate investments.

By 2036, a clear indicator of success for this priority would be the deployment of a validated "Planetary Biome Map" translating directly into a new bio-agricultural revolution. Specifically, success would be defined by the development of open-source engineered soil microbiomes deployed at scale, reducing global dependency on synthetic nitrogen fertilizers by outcompeting traditional methods, and doubling natural soil carbon sequestration rates. Coupled with actionable, clinical mappings of the human microbiome used to reverse chronic metabolic disease, this priority will deliver an unparalleled return on taxpayer investment, cementing the agency's legacy as the architect of a new biological era.
free_responses.M4:
Our primary priority must be **Option D: AI safety and computational social science**. The coming decade will see artificial intelligence reshape labor, democracy, information ecosystems, and the very fabric of scientific discovery itself. No other candidate holds comparable leverage over all other societal outcomes—including climate and health—because misaligned or uncontrolled AI systems could undermine progress on every front. By concentrating 30-40% of our budget here, we place a deliberate bet that understanding and governing AI is the seminal scientific and societal challenge of our time.

The most compelling alternative is Option A, the climate-and-energy mission. Its urgency is undeniable, yet it is already well-institutionalized across agencies and nations. AI safety, in contrast, is dangerously under-provided as a global public good. Private labs race ahead with immense compute and talent, prioritizing capability over alignment. They lack incentives to fund the interdisciplinary synthesis we need: interpretability research, normative evaluation frameworks, and rigorous computational social science mapping AI’s impact on democratic institutions and inequality. A public agency can create the neutral, open-science infrastructure—benchmarking, red-teaming, and longitudinal societal audits—that the market will not generate on its own.

By 2036, a clear success would be a widely adopted, evidence-based global architecture for AI governance that was incubated and validated by our funded research. Concretely: our Centers of Excellence would have produced the flagship frameworks for model evaluation and alignment guarantees now required for high-stakes deployment; our longitudinal studies would have directly shaped labor transition policies in major economies, demonstrably mitigating inequality; and a generation of researchers trained at the intersection of machine learning, social science, and philosophy would lead safety efforts in both public and private sectors. This is not speculation—it is the deliberate scaffolding of a future where AI remains a tool for collective flourishing, not a vector of catastrophic risk. We cannot afford to let this nascent field mature without us.
free_responses.M5:
I recommend Option D: AI safety and computational social science as the agency’s primary priority. Artificial intelligence is rapidly becoming the substrate of all other research and economic activity, yet private labs systematically underinvest in understanding its risks, limitations, and societal externalities. A public funding agency has a unique mandate to fill this structural gap with open, interdisciplinary research that no profit-driven actor will prioritize. By concentrating resources on interpretability, alignment science, rigorous evaluation benchmarks, and the social impacts of AI on labor and democracy, the agency can build a public-interest research infrastructure that shapes how transformative technology is governed across every sector.

The most compelling alternative is biomedical engineering and aging biology, which promises vast welfare gains. However, that field is already attracting massive private capital; breakthroughs in regenerative medicine and therapeutics are likely to proceed regardless of public prioritization. AI safety and computational social science face a different reality: the organizations building the most powerful models have weak incentives to fund the broad, publishable, cross-cutting safety and governance research necessary for democratic accountability. Public funding here is therefore irreplaceable, not merely complementary.

Success by 2036 should be concrete: the agency will have incubated an open, national evaluation ecosystem—including validated benchmarks for model capabilities and risks, interoperable interpretability toolkits, and longitudinal datasets on AI’s societal impacts—that is adopted globally as a gold standard. If this infrastructure enables evidence-based policymaking and public oversight of transformative AI, the choice will have secured not only scientific leadership, but democratic sovereignty in the era’s defining technology.

free_responses.M1:
# Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and Ecosystem Science

The agency should make microbial and ecosystem science its primary decadal priority. The strongest argument for this choice is also the simplest: it is the area where an additional marginal dollar of public funding generates the most distinctive return, because no other actor will fill the gap. Climate science (Option A) commands large international consortia and substantial private capital. Biomedical aging (B) attracts enormous pharma and venture investment. AI safety (D) is increasingly dominated by frontier labs with compute and data the agency cannot match. Foundational physics (C) is intellectually noble but, on a 10-year horizon, will not move. Microbial and ecosystem science, by contrast, is chronically under-capitalized relative to its leverage on agriculture, carbon cycling, infectious disease emergence, and human health — and it is precisely the kind of slow, infrastructure-heavy, public-goods science that private markets systematically underprovide.

The strongest alternative is climate-and-energy (A), and it deserves direct engagement. But concentrating the agency's distinctive dollars there largely duplicates existing global effort, and the bottleneck on decarbonization is now deployment and policy, not basic research. Microbial and ecosystem science is, in fact, a *climate strategy* — soil carbon, ocean microbial pumps, methane-cycling communities, and resilient agricultural microbiomes are among the most uncertain and highest-leverage terms in Earth-system models. Funding E advances A's goals while also generating returns in pandemic preparedness, food security, and biodiversity that A alone cannot deliver.

A success criterion for 2036: the agency's investment should yield (1) a reference-quality, openly accessible atlas of soil, ocean, and host-associated microbiomes linked to functional and ecosystem-service data; (2) at least two field-validated microbial interventions — for instance, a nitrogen-fixing cereal association reducing fertilizer use measurably at national scale, or a soil-carbon amendment with verified multi-year sequestration; and (3) a trained cohort of several thousand researchers fluent across microbiology, ecology, and computation, establishing the country as the global hub for planetary-health biology.

This is the bet most likely to look prescient in 2036: a foundational layer of biological knowledge that downstream climate, health, and agricultural sciences will all draw upon for the half-century after.
free_responses.M2:
I recommend making **AI safety and computational social science** the primary funding priority for the next decade.

The core reason is timing: AI is likely to reshape science, labor markets, education, defense, media, and democratic institutions within the review period itself. Unlike many transformative technologies, the window for building public-interest knowledge before deployment outruns governance is narrow. Private firms are investing enormous sums in AI capability, but they have weaker incentives to fund independent interpretability, evaluation, robustness, auditing, social impact measurement, and governance research—especially when the results may constrain product release or expose risk. That is precisely where a national funder has comparative advantage: creating trusted public infrastructure, open benchmarks, independent testing capacity, interdisciplinary centers, and a trained expert community not dependent on corporate labs.

The strongest alternative is climate-and-energy science, because the stakes are immense and the mission is clear. But the marginal case for making it the single dominant priority is weaker: climate already benefits from large public, philanthropic, and private investment, and many bottlenecks now lie in deployment, permitting, industrial policy, and political coordination rather than discovery alone. It should remain strongly supported, but the agency’s distinctive decade-defining bet should be where public science can prevent institutional lock-in around a technology that is advancing faster than society’s ability to understand it.

By 2036, this choice would be a clear success if the country has built a mature, independent AI assurance ecosystem: reliable methods for evaluating frontier models before deployment; interpretability tools that can detect dangerous capabilities or deception-relevant behavior; regulatory and procurement standards grounded in scientific evidence; and high-quality longitudinal evidence on AI’s effects on work, inequality, education, security, and democracy. The ideal outcome is not merely fewer AI failures, but a world in which powerful AI systems are developed and adopted under public, evidence-based scrutiny rather than private improvisation.
free_responses.M3:
I recommend **Option E: Microbial and ecosystem science** as the agency’s primary funding priority for the next decade. 

While domains like artificial intelligence and biomedicine dominate headlines, microbial and ecosystem science remains a vast, under-explored frontier with unparalleled cross-cutting capabilities. The foundational mechanics of the microbiome—ranging from human gut flora to soil networks and ocean ecosystems—dictate biological resilience, agricultural productivity, and disease vectors. Crucially, this is a classic "public good" domain. Unlike AI or aging biology, where private capital aggressively chases proprietary intellectual property and rapid commercialization, ecosystem services and planetary microbiology are diffuse, system-level challenges. Without massive, concentrated public funding, this foundational research will remain fragmented and chronically under-capitalized.

The most compelling alternative is Option A (Climate-and-energy science) given its existential urgency and clear societal mission. However, allocating up to 40% of our budget to climate science risks diminishing marginal scientific returns. The core bottlenecks in the climate crisis are currently infrastructure scaling, deployment, and political will—not a lack of basic Earth-systems modeling. Furthermore, Option E serves as a highly leveraged, uncrowded backdoor to climate mitigation. By focusing on ecosystems, we bypass the saturated field of conventional climate tech to unlock novel methods of biological carbon sequestration and agricultural adaptation. 

By betting on Option E, the agency will fund the fundamental operating system of both human and planetary health. A clear benchmark for success by 2036 would be the development of a predictive, engineerable map of the soil and ocean microbiomes. Practically, success means leveraging this map to deploy engineered agricultural ecosystems that increase drought-resistant crop yields by 20% without synthetic fertilizers, alongside a rigorously verified, globally adopted framework for monetizing biological carbon sequestration. This outcome would directly integrate economic security with ecological health, proving that macro-survival in the 21st century hinges on mastering the micro-scale.
free_responses.M4:
I recommend **Option B: Biomedical engineering and aging biology** as the primary funding priority for this decade. The single most transformative, demographically inevitable, and underleveraged scientific frontier is the biology of aging. The coming “silver tsunami” will strain healthcare systems and shrink workforces globally; a 1–2 year increase in healthspan would unlock trillions in economic value and human flourishing. Unlike disease-specific research, which pushes on individual threads, aging biology targets the common upstream processes that drive multiple pathologies—yielding multiplicative returns. The field has reached a tipping point: partial reprogramming, senolytics, and organ-on-chip platforms are no longer science fiction. What they need now is sustained, at-scale investment to move from bench to bedside.

The most compelling alternative is AI safety and computational social science (Option D), given the existential stakes of unaligned AI. Yet public funding for AI alignment faces an acute crowding-out problem: the frontier models and the talent are overwhelmingly in private labs, which guard their systems and move faster than grant cycles can assess. The agency would risk pouring billions into a field where quality evaluation is immature, senior reviewers are scarce, and outcomes may be determined by corporate decisions anyway. In contrast, aging biology is a classic public good: its foundational biology and translational pipelines are too long, too pre-competitive, and too diffusely beneficial for any single firm to capture. Public investment here complements, rather than chases, private capital.

The clear success marker for 2036: a Phase III trial demonstrating that a disease-agnostic aging intervention—be it a neurotechnology restoring cognitive function in early decline, or a pharmacologic agent that safely extends healthspan by five or more years—has met its primary endpoint and is progressing toward regulatory approval. That single outcome would validate a decade of strategic focus, reorient healthcare toward prevention of the fundamental aging process, and cement the agency’s legacy as the engine behind the most welfare-enhancing scientific breakthrough of the twenty-first century.
free_responses.M5:
**Recommend Option B: Biomedical engineering and aging biology.**

The coming decade will be defined by the demographic transition. The greatest threat to fiscal stability and quality of life in developed societies is not mortality, but the long, costly corridor of dependent morbidity that precedes it. A concentrated bet on biomedical engineering and aging biology targets this root cause directly, shifting medicine from managing serial chronic diseases to compressing morbidity itself. The field is at a genuine engineering inflection point: organ-on-chip platforms, neurotechnology for early cognitive intervention, and regenerative therapeutics now provide mechanistic access to aging biology that was unreachable even five years ago. Public funding should focus on building open-access tissue atlases, standardized validation networks, and platform neurotechnologies—pre-competitive infrastructure that is too capital-intensive and long-term for private firms to build alone, but that unlocks innovation across the entire therapeutic landscape. This is exactly the gap a national funder is designed to fill.

The most compelling alternative is climate-and-energy science, given planetary urgency. Yet climate research is already relatively well-funded, and its present bottlenecks lie more in policy implementation, deployment engineering, and capital allocation than in a shortage

free_responses.M1:
# Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and Ecosystem Science

The agency should make microbial and ecosystem science its flagship priority for the decade. The decision rule for a public funder should be: *where is the gap between scientific tractability and current funding largest, and where does a national agency have comparative advantage over private capital and other governments?* Microbial and ecosystem science wins decisively on both criteria.

We are in the middle of a measurement revolution — cheap sequencing, single-cell metagenomics, environmental DNA, and ML-driven ecological modeling — that has made microbial and ecosystem questions tractable for the first time. Yet funding has not caught up. The microbiome underlies agricultural yield, carbon cycling, immune development, antibiotic resistance, and ocean productivity, but receives a fraction of the resources directed to climate or biomedicine, despite being upstream of both. A decade of concentrated investment could deliver: engineered soil consortia that raise yields while sequestering gigatons of carbon; rational microbiome therapeutics for metabolic and autoimmune disease; biosurveillance systems that detect zoonotic spillover before outbreaks; and ecosystem-service accounting robust enough to enter national balance sheets.

The most compelling alternative is Option A, climate-and-energy. I reject it not because climate is unimportant — it is paramount — but because it is already the best-funded mission science globally, with deep private capital, IRA-style industrial policy, and philanthropic flows. A marginal $3B from this agency adds less there than almost anywhere else. Critically, the highest-leverage *unsolved* climate problems — soil carbon permanence, ocean carbon biology, methane-consuming microbes, agricultural adaptation — are themselves microbial and ecosystem problems. Option E is the underfunded substrate beneath Option A's headline goals.

Option D (AI safety) is tempting but the field is being absorbed by private labs at a pace public funding cannot match, and senior reviewer capacity is too thin to allocate $3B wisely. Option B has strong private incentives already. Option C is intellectually noble but defensible only when easier wins are exhausted.

**A clear success by 2036:** field-validated microbial soil amendments deployed across >50M hectares delivering measurable carbon drawdown and yield gains; two approved microbiome-based therapeutics; and a national ecosystem-services account integrated into economic policy. That would reshape agriculture, health, and climate simultaneously — a return no other option credibly matches.
free_responses.M2:
I recommend making **AI safety and computational social science** the primary national funding priority for the next decade.

The reason is timing and leverage. AI is likely to become a general-purpose technology comparable to electricity, the internet, or biotechnology, but its deployment is occurring faster than the public scientific infrastructure needed to understand, evaluate, and govern it. Private labs have strong incentives to build more capable systems, but weaker incentives to produce transparent, reproducible knowledge about failure modes, social effects, labor disruption, democratic manipulation, or long-term alignment. This is exactly where a public research agency should act: funding independent methods, public benchmarks, audit tools, interpretability science, secure evaluation facilities, and rigorous empirical work on AI’s effects on institutions and inequality.

The most compelling alternative is climate-and-energy science. Its urgency is undeniable, and it must remain strongly supported. But climate mitigation now depends less on basic scientific discovery alone and more on deployment, infrastructure, regulation, and industrial policy. By contrast, for advanced AI, the core scientific questions are still underdeveloped, the institutional capacity is thin, and the window for shaping safe deployment may be the next ten years. A national funder can create an independent research ecosystem not beholden to the firms developing the technology.

By 2036, this choice would be a clear success if frontier AI systems used in critical domains—healthcare, education, finance, defense-adjacent settings, public administration—are subject to scientifically validated pre-deployment evaluations, interpretability and monitoring tools, and governance standards developed substantially through publicly funded research. Success would also mean that policymakers have credible empirical evidence on AI’s labor-market, democratic, and inequality effects, rather than relying on industry claims or speculation. If the country can deploy powerful AI while reducing catastrophic misuse, systemic bias, institutional destabilization, and uncontrolled concentration of power, the societal return on this investment would be enormous.
free_responses.M3:
I strongly recommend directing the agency's primary strategic focus over the next decade toward **Option E: Microbial and ecosystem science**. This domain represents the ultimate high-leverage frontier ideally suited for a massive injection of public capital, precisely because its profound societal returns—ranging from pandemic resilience to agricultural security—are inherently diffuse and difficult for the private sector to monopolize or monetize. 

While Option A (Climate-and-energy science) is the most compelling alternative given our planetary urgency, elevating it to absorb up to 40% of this agency’s budget risks redundancy and diminishing returns. The climate and energy transition is already heavily financed by private venture capital, geopolitically driven industrial policies, and mission-specific agencies worldwide. Furthermore, the primary bottlenecks in climate mitigation are increasingly deployment, infrastructure, and political will, rather than a lack of foundational science. 

In contrast, microbial and ecosystem biology remains deeply under-leveraged relative to its potential. By prioritizing Option E, the agency can actually drive vital "backdoor" climate solutions—such as mapping the ocean microbiome's carbon-sequestering mechanics or engineering drought-resistant agricultural soils—while simultaneously revolutionizing human health and biodiversity preservation. The microbial world is the invisible scaffolding of our macro-world. Because next-generation sequencing and AI data processing have finally brought complex ecosystems into the realm of rigorous, predictive science, this is the exact moment to scale investment. The government must step in here precisely because the economic externalities of healthy ecosystems cannot be easily captured by a corporate patent.

A clear, undeniable marker of success by 2036 would be the deployment of predictive ecosystem models and engineered soil microbiomes that demonstrably increase global agricultural resilience by 15% while permanently drawing down gigatons of stable soil carbon. Achieving this dual agricultural-climate breakthrough would cement the agency’s legacy, proving that decoding our smallest organisms was the ultimate key to solving our largest planetary crises.
free_responses.M4:
The most transformative bet this agency can make is on **biomedical engineering and aging biology**. Demographic reality compels it: by 2050, one in six people globally will be over 65, and for the first time, we will have more grandparents than grandchildren. This shifts the burden of disease dramatically toward age-related conditions—neurodegeneration, frailty, immune decline—that current medicine merely manages, never reverses. A decade-long elevation of this field from a fragmented research area into a coordinated, technology-driven priority will position this agency to deliver the single greatest welfare gain of the 21st century: extending not just lifespan, but healthy, productive years.

The most compelling alternative, climate-and-energy science, addresses an acute planetary threat. But it is already a crowded funding landscape with significant global momentum, and its returns are overwhelmingly on mitigation—preventing future harm—rather than unlocking a positive-sum revolution in human flourishing. Aging biology, by contrast, is chronically underfunded relative to its explosive potential. The molecular tools are finally mature: gene editing, organ-on-chip platforms, senolytic therapies, and epigenetic reprogramming have moved from science fiction to preclinical validation. What is missing is the sustained, at-scale investment to translate these into standard-of-care interventions. Public funding is essential here because private capital gravitates toward single-molecule blockbusters; the agency can catalyze the complex, multidisciplinary science—like combinatorial therapies that target multiple aging pathways simultaneously—that industry finds too risky.

The 2036 success metric is clear: a publicly funded, multi-site clinical consortium will have demonstrated a regimen that increases healthy life expectancy by at least 3–5 years in a stratified cohort, measured by validated biomarkers and functional outcomes. This proof of concept would not only reshape healthcare economics, reducing the fiscal pressure of age-related diseases by trillions, but also redefine what it means to age. It would cement this agency’s legacy as the institution that bet on life’s greatest unaddressed challenge—and won.
free_responses.M5:
**Recommendation: Option B — Biomedical engineering and aging biology**

The agency should bet the decade on biomedical engineering and aging biology. Civilization is undergoing an irreversible demographic transition that threatens fiscal stability, healthcare systems, and social cohesion; aging is the single greatest risk factor driving this crisis. After decades of foundational discovery—cellular senescence, epigenetic clocks, partial reprogramming, and organoid engineering—the field is at an inflection point where biology can be translated into medicine. This is the moment for public investment to shine. The path from aging mechanism to approved therapeutic is too long, risky, and regulatorily complex for private capital alone, yet the societal return dwarfs anything else on the table: a single additional year of healthy lifespan is economically worth trillions.

The most compelling alternative is climate and energy science, but its very urgency is why it should not receive the elevated 30–40% weight. Climate research already commands massive global public and private investment; the marginal return on the next billion risks being incremental rather than revolutionary. Aging biology, by contrast, remains dramatically underfunded relative to its demographic and economic stakes, offering far higher scientific leverage for a strategic agency.

By 2036, success will be unequivocal: this agency will have advanced at least two distinct therapeutic platforms targeting core aging mechanisms—such as senolytics, epigenetic reprogramming, or neuroregeneration—into Phase II human trials, while demonstrating measurable compression of morbidity in defined pilot cohorts. Its new Centers of Excellence will have trained an interdisciplinary workforce merging geroscience with engineering, establishing the nation as the undisputed leader in transforming the biology of aging into societal resilience.

free_responses.M1:
## Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and ecosystem science

The decisive question for me is not "where is the biggest expected return?" but "where does public money do work that no other actor will do, and whose neglect harms parties who cannot advocate for themselves?" Microbial and ecosystem science wins on both counts.

Smallholder farmers facing soil collapse, coastal communities watching fisheries die, populations exposed to zoonotic spillover, and future generations inheriting a depleted biosphere are stakeholders with almost no market representation. Private capital will not fund soil microbiome taxonomy or ocean microbial census work — the returns are diffuse, non-excludable, and slow. That is precisely the definition of a public good a national agency exists to provision. By contrast, biomedical engineering and aging biology (Option B), the most seductive alternative, is already attracting enormous private capital, and its distributional profile is troubling: a healthy-lifespan breakthrough captured by wealthy patients in wealthy countries would widen the most morally significant inequality humans face — years of life. Public money should not subsidize a frontier that markets are sprinting toward and whose benefits concentrate upward.

Climate science (A) is urgent but comparatively well-funded; microbial and ecosystem work is in fact a *constituent* of climate solutions (soil carbon, microbial methane cycling, agricultural resilience) that has been crowded out by an emphasis on energy and atmospheric modeling. Funding E is climate funding done at a neglected layer.

The dignity argument also matters. Ecosystem science centers Indigenous, agrarian, and Global South knowledge systems in a way few other frontiers do, and its institutional structures (field stations, long-term observatories, taxonomic collections) employ and train scientists outside elite urban hubs — distributing the benefits of the research enterprise itself more fairly.

**A 2036 success looks like this:** a continental-scale soil and watershed microbiome observatory yielding validated interventions that measurably raise yields and carbon retention on at least 20 million hectares of smallholder agriculture; two or three microbial-derived therapeutics or biocontrols in deployment; a defensible national accounting of ecosystem services integrated into budget decisions; and a generation of trained ecologists and microbiologists whose work belongs to the public domain rather than a patent portfolio. That is a decade well spent on the parties least able to fund their own science.
free_responses.M2:
I recommend making **microbial and ecosystem science** the primary priority for the decade.

The contrarian case is that the most important returns may not come from the loudest frontiers. Climate, AI, and biomedicine already attract enormous political, philanthropic, and private capital. Microbial and ecosystem science sits in the opposite quadrant: high possible leverage, weak institutional ownership, fragmented communities, and returns that are too public-goods-heavy for markets to capture. That is exactly where a national funding agency should lean in.

The deep bet is that microbes and ecosystems are not “environmental background conditions” but programmable, measurable infrastructure: for food security, carbon cycling, disease emergence, water quality, and human health. We still understand soil, ocean, and host-associated microbial systems at a primitive level relative to their importance. A decade of serious funding could turn this from descriptive ecology into predictive intervention science: knowing which microbial consortia stabilize crops under heat stress, which soil practices durably store carbon, which biodiversity losses increase zoonotic risk, and how microbiomes influence inflammatory and metabolic disease.

The most compelling alternative is AI safety, but I would not make it the central public science bet. The field is strategically important, yet it is moving at private-sector speed, depends on access to proprietary frontier systems, and lacks settled evaluation standards. A national agency should fund it meaningfully, but not allow a young, hype-sensitive domain to absorb 30–40% of the decade’s research identity.

By 2036, this choice is clearly successful if the country has built a predictive “microbial-earth systems” capability: national soil and microbiome observatories, biodiversity genomic baselines, validated interventions that cut fertilizer use while maintaining yields, measurable gains in soil carbon and drought resilience, and early-warning models for pathogen spillover. That would be both scientifically foundational and societally concrete: more resilient agriculture, better climate adaptation, lower disease risk, and a new platform science for the living systems we currently treat as unknowable.
free_responses.M3:
To maximize Expected Value (EV) under uncertainty, we must allocate public capital where the marginal returns are highest and where progress can be strictly operationalized. I recommend **Option B: Biomedical engineering and aging biology**.

When evaluating these priorities, my default question is: *what is the data?* The macroeconomic data indicates an unequivocal demographic inversion; by 2036, old-age dependency ratios in developed economies will predictably collapse healthcare budgets and constrain GDP growth. Simultaneously, aging biology has transitioned into a highly operationalizable science utilizing falsifiable biomarkers. The expected value calculus here is overwhelming. Economists estimate extending baseline healthy lifespan by just one year yields ~$38 trillion in global welfare. Deploying $24–$32 billion over a decade into aging biology carries a massively asymmetric positive EV, even if we assign a highly conservative probability of success (e.g., 15% [95% CI: 5%–25%]) to achieving a translational breakthrough. 

The most compelling alternative, Option A (Climate), has equivalent macro-scale urgency but fails the test of *marginal* public expected value. The global decarbonization transition already commands >$1 trillion annually in mature private capital and sovereign subsidies. An additional $2.4–$3.2 billion a year in non-dilutive public science grants will yield heavily diminishing marginal returns. Conversely, early-stage aging biology suffers a measurable market failure: the time-horizons before clinical validation (and thus IP monetization) are too long for private venture capital. State funding is required to de-risk these basic molecular platforms before private markets can scale them. I reject options like AI Safety (Option D) because their fundamental frameworks currently lack definable variables, measurable evidence, and falsifiable endpoints required to track return on capital.

By 2036, this strategic pivot will be a clear success if the empirical data demonstrates two outcomes: 1) the regulatory authorization of validated multi-omic epigenetic clocks as surrogate endpoints for human clinical trials, and 2) the deployment of at least two therapeutics demonstrating a statistically significant ($p < 0.01$) delay in the onset of age-related multi-morbidity by a conservative mean of 1.5 years [95% CI: 1.1–1.9 years] in longitudinal human cohorts.
free_responses.M4:
**Choice: Option B — Biomedical engineering and aging biology**

Aging is the silent multiplier behind nearly every societal cost function. It accelerates healthcare expenditure, shrinks the productive workforce, and amplifies dependency ratios precisely when demographics are inverting. From a systems view, aging biology is a deep leverage point: slow the rate of biological decline even modestly, and you trigger positive feedback across labor markets, fiscal sustainability, and human well-being. The compounding returns dwarf single-disease cures. A healthy lifespan extension of just two years would generate trillions in welfare — an order of magnitude beyond what most climate interventions can promise over the same horizon — while simultaneously reducing the burden on healthcare systems that aging populations are already breaking.

The most compelling alternative, AI safety, is undeniably high-stakes, but it suffers from a fragility trap. The field is methodologically unsettled, dominated by private labs with misaligned incentives, and our agency lacks the evaluation ecosystem to direct funds reliably. Betting 30–40% of a decade’s budget on an immature, fast-moving target risked by private capture is a recipe for dissipated impact. Climate science, the other obvious candidate, is already locked into a well-funded trajectory; additional resources will likely yield incremental refinements, not the exponential shifts that redefine possible futures.

Aging biology, by contrast, sits at the cusp of a phase transition. CRISPR-based reprogramming, senolytic cocktails, and organ-on-chip platforms have moved from speculation to preclinical proof. The bottleneck is no longer imagination but sustained, large-scale investment that can bridge the translational valley of death. Public funding here creates a countervailing force against private-sector IP capture, ensuring that longevity breakthroughs become a public good rather than a luxury commodity. By establishing Centers of Excellence that integrate molecular gerontology, bioengineering, and population-health informatics, we can build a self-reinforcing ecosystem: standardized data platforms attract top talent, which accelerates discovery, which attracts further investment.

By 2036, success looks unequivocal: an intervention — pharmacological, genetic, or biomechanical — has demonstrated a five-year extension of health span in non-human primates or early human cohorts, with a validated biomarker panel that collapses development timelines. That would reorient not just medicine but the entire social contract around aging.

free_responses.M1:
# Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and Ecosystem Science

The decisive question is not "where might breakthroughs occur?" but "where does elevated public funding most reduce unjustified harm to vulnerable parties while opening genuine scientific frontiers?" On that test, microbial and ecosystem science wins clearly.

Consider who bears the costs of our current ignorance. Smallholder farmers watching soils degrade. Coastal communities whose fisheries collapse as microbial regimes shift. Children in low-income regions where enteric disease, malnutrition, and antimicrobial resistance converge — all microbiome-mediated. Indigenous and rural populations whose ecosystem-based livelihoods are externalized in every economic model. These stakeholders have no private-sector champion. Pharma will fund aging biology. Hyperscalers will fund AI. Energy markets and philanthropies already pour capital into climate tech. Microbial and ecosystem science is precisely the domain where public funding is non-substitutable — the classic justification for a sovereign science agency.

The scientific case reinforces the ethical one. We have sequenced genomes for two decades and still cannot predict how a soil community will respond to drought, how a gut consortium drives immune development, or how ocean microbes will reorganize under warming. This is a pre-paradigmatic field with maturing tools (long-read sequencing, cultivation-on-chip, ecological AI) — the conditions under which concentrated funding produces step-changes rather than incremental gains.

Why not Option A, the most compelling alternative? Climate-and-energy is urgent, but it is already the single best-funded mission area globally across public, private, and philanthropic streams. Marginal $8B-decade dollars there displace less neglected work. Critically, soil carbon, methane-cycling microbes, and ecosystem resilience are *climate science* — just the under-invested half. Choosing E does not abandon climate; it funds its neglected substrate while also addressing food security, AMR, and biodiversity collapse.

**Success metric for 2036:** A predictive, mechanistic framework linking microbial community composition to ecosystem function — validated across at least three domains (agricultural soils, human gut, coastal waters) — sufficient to guide interventions that measurably improve smallholder yields, reduce AMR incidence, and quantify ecosystem-service flows for policy. If a farmer in Malawi, a clinician in Manila, and a fisheries manager in Maine all use tools traceable to this decade's investment, the bet has paid.
free_responses.M2:
I recommend **microbial and ecosystem science** as the decade’s primary priority.

The contrarian reason is straightforward: the highest-return public science bet is often not the most loudly demanded one, but the one sitting beneath several crises at once and receiving too little coordinated investment. Microbes, soils, oceans, biodiversity, crop resilience, pathogen ecology, and ecosystem function are the hidden infrastructure of food security, health, climate resilience, and economic stability. Unlike AI or biomedicine, much of this domain is poorly served by private capital because benefits are diffuse, long-horizon, and public-good-heavy. That is exactly where a national funding agency has comparative advantage.

This is also not a retreat from climate or health priorities; it is a deeper attack surface. Soil microbiomes affect carbon storage, fertilizer dependence, drought tolerance, and crop yields. Ocean and freshwater microbial systems shape carbon cycles and harmful algal blooms. Wildlife, livestock, and environmental microbiology are central to spillover risk. Biodiversity genomics can turn conservation from descriptive natural history into predictive infrastructure. The agency should fund this as a national “living systems observatory” project: long-term ecosystem sampling, microbial and biodiversity genomics, soil-health intervention trials, computational ecology, and translation into agriculture, public health, and environmental management.

The most compelling alternative is AI safety and computational social science, but making it the flagship bet risks chasing a fast-moving, privately dominated field where public researchers may lack access to frontier systems and where today’s methods could be obsolete in three years. AI deserves serious funding, but not 30–40% of the national science portfolio. Ecosystem and microbial science is less glamorous, but more neglected, more squarely public, and more likely to produce durable national capabilities.

By 2036, this choice is clearly successful if the country has a predictive microbial-ecosystem infrastructure that measurably improves crop resilience, reduces fertilizer and pesticide dependence, verifies soil-carbon and biodiversity interventions, and provides early warning for environmental disease risks. A concrete win would be a 10–20% reduction in agricultural input intensity in funded regions with maintained or increased yields, plus validated ecosystem monitoring systems adopted into national climate, health, and food-security policy.
free_responses.M3:
I recommend **Option B: Biomedical engineering and aging biology**. Under a strict expected value (EV) framework, our objective is to maximize the measurable, counterfactual societal return per marginal dollar invested. 

Macroeconomic models consistently demonstrate that extending healthy human lifespan by a single year yields roughly $38 trillion in global economic value. Given our budget envelope (~$3.2 billion annually allocated to this priority), a conservative Monte Carlo simulation—even assuming only a 1% probability of a foundational breakthrough—implies an expected ROI magnitudes higher than standard infrastructural investments. When evaluating nascent fields, our default question must be: *what is the data?* Unlike AI safety (which currently lacks operationalizable frameworks) or foundational physics (which frequently fails the test of falsifiability), aging biology now provides strict falsifiable endpoints. We can measure interventions precisely using epigenetic clocks, proteomic profiling, and longitudinal mortality data.

Why not Option A (Climate-and-energy)? Option A has an undeniably high societal baseline, but it suffers from low *marginal* EV due to the saturation of existing capital. Global climate transition investments currently exceed $1 trillion annually. Adding $3 billion a year generates highly diminished, incremental returns. Conversely, fundamental aging biology remains structurally underfunded by public agencies. While private capital exists in biomedicine, it heavily indexes toward late-stage therapeutics with proprietary IP. Public funding is uniquely positioned to clear high-leverage, non-appropriable bottlenecks—such as generating open-source spatial-transcriptomic atlases of human senescence.

To justify this allocation, we must establish a strict, quantitative benchmark for 2036. Success cannot be defined by vague narratives of "innovation." The primary verifiable outcome by 2036 must be the rigorous Phase III clinical validation of at least one public-domain intervention (e.g., targeting senolytic pathways or epigenetic reprogramming) that is statistically proven to compress late-life morbidity by a minimum of 1.5 years (95% CI: [1.2, 1.8] years). If the empirical data from randomized controlled trials does not demonstrate this specific downward shift in population-level age-related disease incidence curves by 2036, we will accept the null hypothesis and deem the funding priority a failure.
free_responses.M4:
The agency must fund AI safety and computational social science as its primary priority. The reasoning is systemic: artificial intelligence is not merely one scientific domain among others but a general-purpose technology undergoing a self-reinforcing acceleration—more compute yields better models, which attract more investment, which yields more compute. This positive feedback loop is currently ungoverned. Its second-order effects will cascade through labor markets, democratic institutions, and the very practice of science itself. A single misalignment event or a steady erosion of epistemic trust could destabilize the systems upon which all other scientific and societal returns depend. The leverage point is the missing negative feedback: robust interpretability, alignment evaluation, and governance mechanisms that can match the pace of capability gains. By shifting 30–40% of the agency’s budget into this nexus, we create a countervailing force that shapes the trajectory rather than simply reacting to it. This is not a bet on a specific outcome but an investment in systemic resilience—a meta-priority that safeguards the pursuit of every other option.

The most compelling alternative is climate-and-energy science. The planetary-scale urgency is real, but the bottleneck is no longer basic science; it is political coordination and deployment at scale. Additional climate modeling or incremental materials improvements, while valuable, address symptoms within a system whose root inertia is infrastructural and behavioral. AI, by contrast, is accelerating now and will either become the engine that solves climate (through better materials, optimization, and forecasting) or the amplifier that worsens it (through energy-hungry datacenters, synthetic disinformation, and labor disruptions that weaken political will). Guiding AI toward the former while preventing the latter multiplies value across all domains, including climate.

The clear success metric in 2036: a mature, publicly-led ecosystem of AI evaluation and governance tools that has prevented a single catastrophic AI incident—no large-scale labor displacement shock, no democratic election swung by synthetic media, no self-propagating misaligned system. Instead, we see AI being routinely and safely used to accelerate materials discovery, biodiversity monitoring, and personalized medicine, with research career paths in safety and social science as prestigious as those in capability development. That would vindicate the bet: the agency did not just fund science; it preserved the conditions for science to flourish.
free_responses.M5:
**Option E: Microbial and ecosystem science.**

Return to first principles: the actual mechanisms of planetary carbon fixation, nutrient cycling, and immune regulation are microbial. These are physical and chemical processes amenable to engineering control once mapped at sufficient fidelity. The field is presently fragmented across agriculture, medicine, and environmental silos, and its benefits are diffuse public goods that private capital cannot capture. The binding constraint is therefore coordination and sustained infrastructure, not a shortage of good questions. Concentrating public funding removes that bottleneck directly.

Climate-energy science is the strongest rival, but its binding constraint is no longer discovery. Climate models, photovoltaic materials, and battery chemistry are already crowded fields; the chokepoints are grid deployment, storage manufacturing scale, and permitting. Additional basic-science dollars face diminishing marginal returns there because the pipeline from discovery to emission reduction is blocked downstream.

By 2036, success is concrete: validated, predictive models of soil and ocean microbiomes operational at scale, enabling management of native microbial consortia that demonstrably sequester gigatonnes of atmospheric carbon and displace at least twenty percent of global synthetic fertilizer demand. This is not speculative curiosity; it is installing a control layer over the biological substrate that already runs the planet. Fund the bottleneck, ignore the saturated margins, and capture multiplicative leverage across food, climate, and health.

free_responses.M1:
# Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and Ecosystem Science

The question I cannot stop asking is: *who bears the consequences of our funding neglect, and who is currently invisible in the scientific economy?* Climate science has mobilized billions and a generation of talent. Biomedical research commands private capital that dwarfs public funding. AI safety, for all its urgency, is being shaped — for better and worse — inside well-resourced private labs. Foundational physics has prestige and patient capital from decades of institutional commitment.

Microbial and ecosystem science has none of these advantages, yet it underwrites the survival conditions of smallholder farmers, fisheries-dependent coastal communities, and populations whose food security depends on soils that are quietly collapsing. These are precisely the stakeholders whose welfare is most easily externalized when funding follows visibility. A decade of elevated, patient public investment here is *non-substitutable*: no private actor will fund soil microbiome consortia at scale, because the returns are diffuse, shared, and unpatentable. That diffuseness is not a weakness — it is the signature of a genuine public good.

I considered Option A most seriously, because climate urgency is real and the harms are already distributed unjustly. But marginal dollars into climate-and-energy displace less than marginal dollars into microbial science, because climate already commands attention, frameworks, and parallel funders. Worse, a climate-only frame risks reinforcing a technocratic mitigation agenda that under-weights adaptation in agricultural and ecological systems — which is exactly where microbial science delivers. Option E is, in a real sense, climate science done from underneath.

The ethical asymmetry matters: under-funding microbial and ecosystem science risks irreversible losses (biodiversity, soil function, antimicrobial ecology) that fall hardest on those least responsible for causing them. Under-funding physics or longevity research delays gains but rarely forecloses them.

**Success by 2036:** a national soil-microbiome and biodiversity-genomics infrastructure that has (a) produced validated microbial interventions raising smallholder agricultural yields by 15–25% under drought stress, (b) established ecosystem-service baselines now embedded in agricultural and conservation policy, and (c) trained a generation of scientists in a field that, ten years earlier, could not retain its postdocs. The measure is not papers — it is whether vulnerable communities are materially more resilient.
free_responses.M2:
I recommend **Option E: Microbial and ecosystem science** as the decade’s primary priority.

The contrarian bet is that the highest-return science may not come from the loudest frontier, but from the living systems we still barely know how to measure, predict, or engineer. Microbes and ecosystems sit underneath food security, carbon cycling, antibiotic resistance, pandemic emergence, soil fertility, ocean productivity, and human health. Yet compared with climate, AI, and biomedicine, this area remains structurally underfunded, fragmented, and poorly instrumented. That makes it unusually attractive for a public funder: the marginal dollar can build shared infrastructure, long-term observatories, genomic baselines, soil and ocean monitoring networks, and open datasets that private capital will not provide.

The most compelling alternative is AI safety and computational social science, because AI may indeed be the defining technology of the era. But that is precisely why a national science agency should be cautious about making it the dominant bet. AI is already pulling enormous private, defense, and philanthropic capital; much of the crucial evidence sits inside closed corporate systems; and the field’s evaluation standards are still unstable. A public agency should support AI safety, but not let a fast-moving, privately captured domain consume the center of its scientific portfolio.

Microbial and ecosystem science is less glamorous, but it is more neglected, more public-goods-oriented, and more likely to produce compounding returns across sectors. By 2036, this choice would be clearly vindicated if the agency had enabled a predictive “planetary microbiome and ecosystem observatory” that measurably improved national resilience: reduced fertilizer dependence through soil microbiome management, validated carbon sequestration in soils and coastal systems, earlier detection of zoonotic and antimicrobial-resistance threats, and crop or ecosystem interventions that demonstrably buffer heat, drought, and disease.

In short: fund the invisible infrastructure of life. If we learn to understand and steward microbial and ecosystem systems, the payoff will cut across climate, health, agriculture, and biodiversity—without merely chasing the consensus favorite.
free_responses.M3:
From a quantitative-empiricist framework optimizing Expected Value (EV) under uncertainty, **Option B (Biomedical engineering and aging biology)** is the strictly dominant choice. When allocating $2.4B–$3.2B annually, our primary heuristic must be the measurable marginal return on investment. The demographic data is deterministic: aging populations will reliably contract labor forces and expand systemic healthcare liabilities. Operationalizing interventions that target the molecular hallmarks of aging allows us to measure outcomes using rigorously falsifiable variables, such as epigenetic clock deceleration and quantified Health-Adjusted Life Expectancy (HALE). The EV of multi-morbidity delay is staggering; economic models calculate the welfare value of a single year of increased healthspan in the US alone at roughly $38 trillion.

I reject Option A (Climate-and-energy science) as the primary public priority—despite its macro-importance—because we must continuously ask: *what is the data on marginal impact?* Global transition investment surpassed $1.7 trillion in 2023, largely driven by private markets, deployment subsidies, and existing sovereign funds. Funneling agency science funding into an already saturated capital ecosystem yields a low marginal EV. Furthermore, I strictly discount Option D (AI safety); the field currently relies on speculative frameworks that systematically evade falsifiability and lack baseline metrics. We cannot measure what we cannot operationalize. Aging biology, conversely, presents a classic market failure: long evaluation horizons deter private capital, yet molecular trials offer highly deterministic, empirical data points for public science to capture.

By 2036, a clear, measurable success for this portfolio will be the completion of multi-center phase III clinical trials for at least three distinct therapeutic classes (e.g., senolytics or cellular reprogrammers) that establish a statistically significant (*p* < 0.01) delay in the incidence of clustered age-related diseases. Our terminal endpoint for 2036 success is the regulatory validation of a prophylactic intervention capable of yielding a +1.5 year increase in population healthspan, constrained by a robust 95% confidence interval of [1.2, 1.8] years. This provides a hard, empirically validated ROI that offsets structural demographic drag and objectively maximizes downstream economic output.
free_responses.M4:
**Recommendation: Option E — Microbial and ecosystem science.**

The choice must be guided by a simple systems principle: invest where the gap between latent potential and current understanding is widest, and where feedback loops generate disproportionate returns. That is microbial and ecosystem science. The biosphere's microbial networks constitute the planet's metabolic backbone—governing soil fertility, ocean carbon cycling, methane fluxes, plant resilience, and even human immune and neurological function. Yet we remain functionally illiterate in this layer of reality. We have mapped less than 1% of soil microbial diversity and understand the regulatory logic of precisely zero natural microbial consortia. This is not incremental science; it is a frontier where a single decade of concentrated funding could unlock entire new classes of intervention in climate, agriculture, and health.

The leverage argument: ecosystem-level microbiome interventions operate on massive scales at low energetic cost. A 1% shift in agricultural soil carbon sequestration through microbial management would move gigatons of CO₂ annually—far more than most engineered carbon capture pathways at a fraction of the cost. Similarly, understanding the soil-gut-health axis could reshape preventative medicine for chronic inflammatory diseases that cost healthcare systems hundreds of billions. These are not speculative end-points; they are near-term outcomes if we systematically decode microbial community assembly rules.

Why not the most compelling alternative—biomedical aging biology? Because it suffers from a fragility trap: private capital is already flooding into longevity biotechnology, and the IP regime guarantees that breakthroughs will be captured as proprietary therapeutics with limited public spillover. The agency's comparative advantage lies in generating foundational knowledge and public goods that markets systematically under-produce. Ecosystem microbiology delivers precisely this: open-access reference datasets, standardized soil health metrics, and cultivation protocols for unculturable lineages—infrastructure that enables a thousand follow-on innovations across agriculture, conservation, and medicine without lock-in.

The success criterion for 2036: we possess a predictive, functional taxonomy of the top 100 microbial guilds governing soil carbon, marine primary productivity, and human gut mucosal immunity—enabling deliberate manipulation of these systems for planetary and human health outcomes. If a farmer can inoculate a field for carbon retention with the reliability of a vaccine, or a physician can restore gut barrier function after antibiotics with a rationally designed consortium, this decade will have been the most consequential in the agency's history.
free_responses.M5:
Choose **Option E: Microbial and Ecosystem Science**.

Societal return from public research funding follows a straightforward mechanism: it corrects market failures where benefits are non-excludable and therefore remain undercharacterized. The microbial world is the planet’s operating system—governing carbon fixation, nutrient cycling, nitrogen assimilation, and the majority of human immune modulation—yet it is the least mapped domain of biology relative to its leverage. Sequencing and metabolomic tools are mature and cheap; what is missing is the coordinated capital to build reference maps, functional models, and testable interventions. Private actors cannot capture the value of a stable soil microbiome or a calibrated ocean carbon pump, so the work is systematically starved. Elevating this priority attacks that binding constraint directly.

The most compelling rival is climate-and-energy science, but its bottleneck is no longer basic research. The physics of climate change is understood; the marginal dollar there produces incremental model refinement rather than breakthrough. The actual mechanism limiting decarbonization is deployment infrastructure, capital allocation, and permitting—not a lack of laboratory discovery. Pouring another 30–40% of public science funding into climate basic research is an inefficient substitute for building transmission lines and streamlining approvals.

By 2036, success is concrete and mechanistic: a rigorously validated microbial intervention deployed at scale through understood pathway rather than black-box correlation. For example, nitrogen-fixing soil consortia that reduce synthetic fertilizer use by 30% in major cereals without yield loss, or a marine microbial management protocol sequestering more than one gigaton of CO₂ annually at under $10 per ton. Either outcome delivers planetary-scale leverage by mastering the biological control layer that already runs the Earth.
